answer: **The poem *Widsith* (chapter 14 of *Old English Elegies*) opens with the scop Widsith introducing himself as a far-traveler among tribes and kings, before reciting a catalog of rulers he claims to have known or heard of.[^1] His speech begins as follows:** > Many men I have heard of who ruled over nations! > Every prince must live according to custom, > one earl after another ruling the land, > he who wishes his throne to thrive. > Of these, Hwala was for a time the best, > and Alexander the most powerful of all > mankind, and he prospered most > of those I have heard of across the earth. > Attila ruled the Huns, Eormanric the Goths, > Becca the Banings, the Burgundians Gifica. > Caesar ruled the Greeks and Celic the Finns, > Hagena the Holm-Rygs and Heoden the Glomms. > Witta ruled the Swæfs, Wada the Hælsings, > Meaca the Myrgings, Mearchealf the Hundings. > Theodric ruled the Franks, Thyle the Rondings, > Breoca the Brondings, Billing the Werns. > Oswine ruled the Eows and Gefwulf the Ytes, > Fin Folcwalding the Frisian kin. > Sigehere ruled the Sea-Danes longest, > Hnæf the Hocings, Helm the Wulfings, > Wald the Woings, Wod the Thuringians, > Sæferth the Sycgs, Ongendtheow the Swedes, > Sceafthere the Ymbers, Sceafa the Longbeards, > Hun the Hætweras and Holen the Wrosns. > Hringweald was the name of the Herefaras' king.[^2] [^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14 (“Widsith matholade, wordhord onleac,”), opening paragraph [p-2f6bd3](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3). [^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, paragraph [p-545201](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-545201). (Translation variant.)
